items.correct: Learning by observing the consequences others receive for behaviour.
items.misconception: Being directly rewarded for copying someone.
items.why: Students confuse vicarious with direct reinforcement. 'Vicarious' means experienced through someone else - you see them rewarded/punished and adjust your own behaviour accordingly.

items.q: Why does the cognitive approach use INFERENCE?
items.correct: Mental processes cannot be directly observed, so they are inferred from behaviour.
items.misconception: Because cognitive psychologists guess instead of measuring.
items.why: Inference is a methodological necessity, not a weakness of rigour. Students treat it as vague guessing. It means drawing conclusions about unobservable processes from observable behaviour.

items.q: How are phobias MAINTAINED according to Mowrer's two-process model?
items.correct: Through operant conditioning - avoidance reduces anxiety (negative reinforcement).
items.misconception: Through classical conditioning continuing over time.
items.why: Classical conditioning ACQUIRES the phobia; operant conditioning MAINTAINS it. Students conflate these. Avoiding the feared object reduces anxiety, reinforcing the avoidance.

items.q: Maguire (2000) found enlarged ___ in London taxi drivers:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Hippocampi. “Amygdala” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
It's 5-HTT and depression. “DRD4 and risk-taking” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year did Maguire publish her London taxi drivers study?
items.why: Exact answer: 2000 AD. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
